lines: Who were those went by?
fullText: Who were those went by?

lines: And whither go they?
fullText: And whither go they?

lines: What was his cause of anger?
fullText: What was his cause of anger?

lines: Good; and what of him?
fullText: Good; and what of him?

lines: So do all men unless they are drunk, sick,
or have no legs.
fullText: So do all men unless they are drunk, sick, or have no legs.

lines: But how should this man that makes me
smile make Hector angry?
fullText: But how should this man that makes me smile make Hector angry?

lines: Who comes here?
fullText: Who comes here?

lines: Hector's a gallant man.
fullText: Hector's a gallant man.

lines: Good morrow, Uncle Pandarus.
fullText: Good morrow, Uncle Pandarus.

lines: This morning, uncle.
fullText: This morning, uncle.

lines: Hector was gone, but Helen was not up.
fullText: Hector was gone, but Helen was not up.

lines: That were we talking of, and of his anger.
fullText: That were we talking of, and of his anger.

lines: So he says here.
fullText: So he says here.

lines: What, is he angry too?
fullText: What, is he angry too?

lines: O Jupiter, there's no comparison.
fullText: O Jupiter, there's no comparison.

lines: Ay, if I ever saw him before and knew him.
fullText: Ay, if I ever saw him before and knew him.

lines: Then you say as I say, for I am sure he is not
Hector.
fullText: Then you say as I say, for I am sure he is not Hector.

lines: 'Tis just to each of them; he is himself.
fullText: 'Tis just to each of them; he is himself.

lines: So he is.
fullText: So he is.

lines: He is not Hector.
fullText: He is not Hector.

lines: Excuse me.
fullText: Excuse me.

lines: Pardon me, pardon me.
fullText: Pardon me, pardon me.

lines: He shall not need it, if he have his own.
fullText: He shall not need it, if he have his own.

lines: No matter.
fullText: No matter.

lines: 'Twould not become him. His own 's better.
fullText: 'Twould not become him. His own 's better.

lines: No, but brown.
fullText: No, but brown.

lines: To say the truth, true and not true.
fullText: To say the truth, true and not true.

lines: Why, Paris hath color enough.
fullText: Why, Paris hath color enough.

lines: Then Troilus should have too much. If she
praised him above, his complexion is higher than
his. He having color enough, and the other higher,
is too flaming a praise for a good complexion. I
had as lief Helen's golden tongue had commended
Troilus for a copper nose.
fullText: Then Troilus should have too much. If she praised him above, his complexion is higher than his. He having color enough, and the other higher, is too flaming a praise for a good complexion. I had as lief Helen's golden tongue had commended Troilus for a copper nose.

lines: Then she's a merry Greek indeed.
fullText: Then she's a merry Greek indeed.

lines: Indeed, a tapster's arithmetic may soon bring
his particulars therein to a total.
fullText: Indeed, a tapster's arithmetic may soon bring his particulars therein to a total.

lines: Is he so young a man and so old a lifter?
fullText: Is he so young a man and so old a lifter?

lines: Juno have mercy! How came it cloven?
fullText: Juno have mercy! How came it cloven?

lines: O, he smiles valiantly.
fullText: O, he smiles valiantly.

lines: O yes, an 'twere a cloud in autumn.
fullText: O yes, an 'twere a cloud in autumn.

lines: Troilus will stand to the proof if you'll
prove it so.
fullText: Troilus will stand to the proof if you'll prove it so.

lines: If you love an addle egg as well as you love
an idle head, you would eat chickens i' th' shell.
fullText: If you love an addle egg as well as you love an idle head, you would eat chickens i' th' shell.

lines: Without the rack.
fullText: Without the rack.

lines: Alas, poor chin! Many a wart is richer.
fullText: Alas, poor chin! Many a wart is richer.

lines: With millstones.
fullText: With millstones.

lines: But there was a more temperate fire under
the pot of her eyes. Did her eyes run o'er too?
fullText: But there was a more temperate fire under the pot of her eyes. Did her eyes run o'er too?

lines: At what was all this laughing?
fullText: At what was all this laughing?

lines: An 't had been a green hair, I should have
laughed too.
fullText: An 't had been a green hair, I should have laughed too.

lines: What was his answer?
fullText: What was his answer?

lines: This is her question.
fullText: This is her question.

lines: So let it now, for it has been a great while
going by.
fullText: So let it now, for it has been a great while going by.

lines: So I do.
fullText: So I do.

lines: And I'll spring up in his tears an 'twere a nettle
against May.
fullText: And I'll spring up in his tears an 'twere a nettle against May.

lines: At your pleasure.
fullText: At your pleasure.

lines: Speak not so loud.
fullText: Speak not so loud.

lines: Who's that?
fullText: Who's that?

lines: Will he give you the nod?
fullText: Will he give you the nod?

lines: If he do, the rich shall have more.
fullText: If he do, the rich shall have more.

lines: O, a brave man!
fullText: O, a brave man!

lines: Be those with swords?
fullText: Be those with swords?

lines: Who's that?
fullText: Who's that?

lines: Can Helenus fight, uncle?
fullText: Can Helenus fight, uncle?

lines: What sneaking fellow comes yonder?
fullText: What sneaking fellow comes yonder?

lines: Peace, for shame, peace.
fullText: Peace, for shame, peace.

lines: Here comes more.
fullText: Here comes more.

lines: There is amongst the Greeks Achilles, a better
man than Troilus.
fullText: There is amongst the Greeks Achilles, a better man than Troilus.

lines: Well, well.
fullText: Well, well.

lines: Ay, a minced man; and then to be baked with
no date in the pie, for then the man's date is out.
fullText: Ay, a minced man; and then to be baked with no date in the pie, for then the man's date is out.

lines: Upon my back to defend my belly, upon my
wit to defend my wiles, upon my secrecy to defend
mine honesty, my mask to defend my beauty, and
you to defend all these; and at all these wards I lie,
at a thousand watches.
fullText: Upon my back to defend my belly, upon my wit to defend my wiles, upon my secrecy to defend mine honesty, my mask to defend my beauty, and you to defend all these; and at all these wards I lie, at a thousand watches.

lines: Nay, I'll watch you for that, and that's one of
the chiefest of them too. If I cannot ward what I
would not have hit, I can watch you for telling how
I took the blow--unless it swell past hiding, and
then it's past watching.
fullText: Nay, I'll watch you for that, and that's one of the chiefest of them too. If I cannot ward what I would not have hit, I can watch you for telling how I took the blow--unless it swell past hiding, and then it's past watching.

lines: Adieu, uncle.
fullText: Adieu, uncle.

lines: To bring, uncle?
fullText: To bring, uncle?

lines: By the same token, you are a bawd.
Words, vows, gifts, tears, and love's full sacrifice
He offers in another's enterprise;
But more in Troilus thousandfold I see
Than in the glass of Pandar's praise may be.
Yet hold I off. Women are angels, wooing;
Things won are done; joy's soul lies in the doing.
That she beloved knows naught that knows not this:
Men prize the thing ungained more than it is.
That she was never yet that ever knew
Love got so sweet as when desire did sue.
Therefore this maxim out of love I teach:
Achievement is command; ungained, beseech.
Then though my heart's content firm love doth bear,
Nothing of that shall from mine eyes appear.
fullText: By the same token, you are a bawd. Words, vows, gifts, tears, and love's full sacrifice He offers in another's enterprise; But more in Troilus thousandfold I see Than in the glass of Pandar's praise may be. Yet hold I off. Women are angels, wooing; Things won are done; joy's soul lies in the doing. That she beloved knows naught that knows not this: Men prize the thing ungained more than it is. That she was never yet that ever knew Love got so sweet as when desire did sue. Therefore this maxim out of love I teach: Achievement is command; ungained, beseech. Then though my heart's content firm love doth bear, Nothing of that shall from mine eyes appear.

lines: Will you walk in, my lord?
fullText: Will you walk in, my lord?

lines: "Wished," my lord? The gods grant--O, my
lord!
fullText: "Wished," my lord? The gods grant--O, my lord!

lines: More dregs than water, if my fears have eyes.
fullText: More dregs than water, if my fears have eyes.

lines: Blind fear, that seeing reason leads, finds
safer footing than blind reason, stumbling without
fear. To fear the worst oft cures the worse.
fullText: Blind fear, that seeing reason leads, finds safer footing than blind reason, stumbling without fear. To fear the worst oft cures the worse.

lines: Nor nothing monstrous neither?
fullText: Nor nothing monstrous neither?

lines: They say all lovers swear more performance
than they are able and yet reserve an ability that
they never perform, vowing more than the perfection
of ten and discharging less than the tenth part
of one. They that have the voice of lions and the
act of hares, are they not monsters?
fullText: They say all lovers swear more performance than they are able and yet reserve an ability that they never perform, vowing more than the perfection of ten and discharging less than the tenth part of one. They that have the voice of lions and the act of hares, are they not monsters?

lines: Will you walk in, my lord?
fullText: Will you walk in, my lord?

lines: Well, uncle, what folly I commit I dedicate
to you.
fullText: Well, uncle, what folly I commit I dedicate to you.

lines: Boldness comes to me now and brings me heart.
Prince Troilus, I have loved you night and day
For many weary months.
fullText: Boldness comes to me now and brings me heart. Prince Troilus, I have loved you night and day For many weary months.

lines: Hard to seem won; but I was won, my lord,
With the first glance that ever--pardon me;
If I confess much, you will play the tyrant.
I love you now, but till now not so much
But I might master it. In faith, I lie;
My thoughts were like unbridled children grown
Too headstrong for their mother. See, we fools!
Why have I blabbed? Who shall be true to us
When we are so unsecret to ourselves?
But though I loved you well, I wooed you not;
And yet, good faith, I wished myself a man;
Or that we women had men's privilege
Of speaking first. Sweet, bid me hold my tongue,
For in this rapture I shall surely speak
The thing I shall repent. See, see, your silence,
Cunning in dumbness, from my weakness draws
My very soul of counsel! Stop my mouth.
fullText: Hard to seem won; but I was won, my lord, With the first glance that ever--pardon me; If I confess much, you will play the tyrant. I love you now, but till now not so much But I might master it. In faith, I lie; My thoughts were like unbridled children grown Too headstrong for their mother. See, we fools! Why have I blabbed? Who shall be true to us When we are so unsecret to ourselves? But though I loved you well, I wooed you not; And yet, good faith, I wished myself a man; Or that we women had men's privilege Of speaking first. Sweet, bid me hold my tongue, For in this rapture I shall surely speak The thing I shall repent. See, see, your silence, Cunning in dumbness, from my weakness draws My very soul of counsel! Stop my mouth.

lines: My lord, I do beseech you pardon me.
'Twas not my purpose thus to beg a kiss.
I am ashamed. O heavens, what have I done!
For this time will I take my leave, my lord.
fullText: My lord, I do beseech you pardon me. 'Twas not my purpose thus to beg a kiss. I am ashamed. O heavens, what have I done! For this time will I take my leave, my lord.

lines: Pray you, content you.
fullText: Pray you, content you.

lines: Sir, mine own company.
fullText: Sir, mine own company.

lines: Let me go and try.
I have a kind of self resides with you,
But an unkind self that itself will leave
To be another's fool. I would be gone.
Where is my wit? I know not what I speak.
fullText: Let me go and try. I have a kind of self resides with you, But an unkind self that itself will leave To be another's fool. I would be gone. Where is my wit? I know not what I speak.

lines: Perchance, my lord, I show more craft than love
And fell so roundly to a large confession
To angle for your thoughts. But you are wise,
Or else you love not; for to be wise and love
Exceeds man's might. That dwells with gods above.
fullText: Perchance, my lord, I show more craft than love And fell so roundly to a large confession To angle for your thoughts. But you are wise, Or else you love not; for to be wise and love Exceeds man's might. That dwells with gods above.

lines: In that I'll war with you.
fullText: In that I'll war with you.

lines: Prophet may you be!
If I be false or swerve a hair from truth,
When time is old and hath forgot itself,
When water drops have worn the stones of Troy
And blind oblivion swallowed cities up,
And mighty states characterless are grated
To dusty nothing, yet let memory,
From false to false, among false maids in love,
Upbraid my falsehood! When they've said "as false
As air, as water, wind or sandy earth,
As fox to lamb, or wolf to heifer's calf,
Pard to the hind, or stepdame to her son,"
Yea, let them say, to stick the heart of falsehood,
"As false as Cressid."
fullText: Prophet may you be! If I be false or swerve a hair from truth, When time is old and hath forgot itself, When water drops have worn the stones of Troy And blind oblivion swallowed cities up, And mighty states characterless are grated To dusty nothing, yet let memory, From false to false, among false maids in love, Upbraid my falsehood! When they've said "as false As air, as water, wind or sandy earth, As fox to lamb, or wolf to heifer's calf, Pard to the hind, or stepdame to her son," Yea, let them say, to stick the heart of falsehood, "As false as Cressid."

lines: Amen.
fullText: Amen.

lines: Then, sweet my lord, I'll call mine uncle down.
He shall unbolt the gates.
fullText: Then, sweet my lord, I'll call mine uncle down. He shall unbolt the gates.

lines: Good morrow, then.
fullText: Good morrow, then.

lines: Are you aweary of me?
fullText: Are you aweary of me?

lines: Night hath been too brief.
fullText: Night hath been too brief.

lines: Prithee, tarry. You men will never tarry.
O foolish Cressid! I might have still held off,
And then you would have tarried. Hark, there's one up.
fullText: Prithee, tarry. You men will never tarry. O foolish Cressid! I might have still held off, And then you would have tarried. Hark, there's one up.

lines: A pestilence on him! Now will he be mocking.
I shall have such a life!
fullText: A pestilence on him! Now will he be mocking. I shall have such a life!

lines: Go hang yourself, you naughty mocking uncle.
You bring me to do--and then you flout me too.
fullText: Go hang yourself, you naughty mocking uncle. You bring me to do--and then you flout me too.

lines: Come, come, beshrew your heart! You'll ne'er be good
Nor suffer others.
fullText: Come, come, beshrew your heart! You'll ne'er be good Nor suffer others.

lines: Did not I tell you? Would he were knocked i' th' head!
Who's that at door?--Good uncle, go and see.--
My lord, come you again into my chamber.
You smile and mock me, as if I meant naughtily.
fullText: Did not I tell you? Would he were knocked i' th' head! Who's that at door?--Good uncle, go and see.-- My lord, come you again into my chamber. You smile and mock me, as if I meant naughtily.

lines: Come, you are deceived. I think of no such thing.
How earnestly they knock! Pray you, come in.
I would not for half Troy have you seen here.
fullText: Come, you are deceived. I think of no such thing. How earnestly they knock! Pray you, come in. I would not for half Troy have you seen here.

lines: How now? What's the matter? Who was here?
fullText: How now? What's the matter? Who was here?

lines: Why sigh you so profoundly? Where's my lord?
Gone? Tell me, sweet uncle, what's the matter?
fullText: Why sigh you so profoundly? Where's my lord? Gone? Tell me, sweet uncle, what's the matter?

lines: O the gods! What's the matter?
fullText: O the gods! What's the matter?

lines: Good uncle, I beseech you, on my knees I
beseech you, what's the matter?
fullText: Good uncle, I beseech you, on my knees I beseech you, what's the matter?

lines: O you immortal gods! I will not go.
fullText: O you immortal gods! I will not go.

lines: I will not, uncle. I have forgot my father.
I know no touch of consanguinity,
No kin, no love, no blood, no soul so near me
As the sweet Troilus. O you gods divine,
Make Cressid's name the very crown of falsehood
If ever she leave Troilus! Time, force, and death
Do to this body what extremes you can,
But the strong base and building of my love
Is as the very center of the Earth,
Drawing all things to it. I'll go in and weep--
fullText: I will not, uncle. I have forgot my father. I know no touch of consanguinity, No kin, no love, no blood, no soul so near me As the sweet Troilus. O you gods divine, Make Cressid's name the very crown of falsehood If ever she leave Troilus! Time, force, and death Do to this body what extremes you can, But the strong base and building of my love Is as the very center of the Earth, Drawing all things to it. I'll go in and weep--

lines: Tear my bright hair, and scratch my praised cheeks,
Crack my clear voice with sobs, and break my heart
With sounding "Troilus." I will not go from Troy.
fullText: Tear my bright hair, and scratch my praised cheeks, Crack my clear voice with sobs, and break my heart With sounding "Troilus." I will not go from Troy.

lines: Why tell you me of moderation?
The grief is fine, full, perfect that I taste,
And violenteth in a sense as strong
As that which causeth it. How can I moderate it?
If I could temporize with my affection
Or brew it to a weak and colder palate,
The like allayment could I give my grief.
My love admits no qualifying dross;
No more my grief in such a precious loss.
fullText: Why tell you me of moderation? The grief is fine, full, perfect that I taste, And violenteth in a sense as strong As that which causeth it. How can I moderate it? If I could temporize with my affection Or brew it to a weak and colder palate, The like allayment could I give my grief. My love admits no qualifying dross; No more my grief in such a precious loss.

lines: O Troilus, Troilus!
fullText: O Troilus, Troilus!

lines: Have the gods envy?
fullText: Have the gods envy?

lines: And is it true that I must go from Troy?
fullText: And is it true that I must go from Troy?

lines: What, and from Troilus too?
fullText: What, and from Troilus too?

lines: Is 't possible?
fullText: Is 't possible?

lines: I must, then, to the Grecians?
fullText: I must, then, to the Grecians?

lines: A woeful Cressid 'mongst the merry Greeks.
When shall we see again?
fullText: A woeful Cressid 'mongst the merry Greeks. When shall we see again?

lines: I true? How now, what wicked deem is this?
fullText: I true? How now, what wicked deem is this?

lines: O, you shall be exposed, my lord, to dangers
As infinite as imminent! But I'll be true.
fullText: O, you shall be exposed, my lord, to dangers As infinite as imminent! But I'll be true.

lines: And you this glove. When shall I see you?
fullText: And you this glove. When shall I see you?

lines: O heavens! "Be true" again?
fullText: O heavens! "Be true" again?

lines: O heavens, you love me not!
fullText: O heavens, you love me not!

lines: Do you think I will?
fullText: Do you think I will?

lines: My lord, will you be true?
fullText: My lord, will you be true?

lines: In kissing, do you render or receive?
fullText: In kissing, do you render or receive?

lines: I'll make my match to live,
The kiss you take is better than you give.
Therefore no kiss.
fullText: I'll make my match to live, The kiss you take is better than you give. Therefore no kiss.

lines: You are an odd man. Give even, or give none.
fullText: You are an odd man. Give even, or give none.

lines: No, Paris is not, for you know 'tis true
That you are odd, and he is even with you.
fullText: No, Paris is not, for you know 'tis true That you are odd, and he is even with you.

lines: No, I'll be sworn.
fullText: No, I'll be sworn.

lines: You may.
fullText: You may.

lines: Why, beg two.
fullText: Why, beg two.

lines: I am your debtor; claim it when 'tis due.
fullText: I am your debtor; claim it when 'tis due.

lines: Now, my sweet guardian. Hark, a word with you.
fullText: Now, my sweet guardian. Hark, a word with you.

lines: Remember? Yes.
fullText: Remember? Yes.

lines: Sweet honey Greek, tempt me no more to folly.
fullText: Sweet honey Greek, tempt me no more to folly.

lines: I'll tell you what--
fullText: I'll tell you what--

lines: In faith, I cannot. What would you have me do?
fullText: In faith, I cannot. What would you have me do?

lines: I prithee, do not hold me to mine oath.
Bid me do anything but that, sweet Greek.
fullText: I prithee, do not hold me to mine oath. Bid me do anything but that, sweet Greek.

lines: Diomed--
fullText: Diomed--

lines: Hark, a word in your ear.
fullText: Hark, a word in your ear.

lines: Nay, but you part in anger.
fullText: Nay, but you part in anger.

lines: Guardian! Why, Greek!
fullText: Guardian! Why, Greek!

lines: In faith, I do not. Come hither once again.
fullText: In faith, I do not. Come hither once again.

lines: In faith, I will, la. Never trust me else.
fullText: In faith, I will, la. Never trust me else.

lines: I'll fetch you one.
fullText: I'll fetch you one.

lines: Here, Diomed. Keep this
sleeve.
fullText: Here, Diomed. Keep this sleeve.

lines: You look upon that sleeve? Behold it well.
He loved me--O false wench!--Give 't me again.
fullText: You look upon that sleeve? Behold it well. He loved me--O false wench!--Give 't me again.

lines: It is no matter, now I ha 't again.
I will not meet with you tomorrow night.
I prithee, Diomed, visit me no more.
fullText: It is no matter, now I ha 't again. I will not meet with you tomorrow night. I prithee, Diomed, visit me no more.

lines: What, this?
fullText: What, this?

lines: O all you gods!--O pretty, pretty pledge!
Thy master now lies thinking on his bed
Of thee and me, and sighs, and takes my glove,
And gives memorial dainty kisses to it
As I kiss thee.
fullText: O all you gods!--O pretty, pretty pledge! Thy master now lies thinking on his bed Of thee and me, and sighs, and takes my glove, And gives memorial dainty kisses to it As I kiss thee.

lines: He that takes that doth take my heart withal.
fullText: He that takes that doth take my heart withal.

lines: You shall not have it, Diomed, faith, you shall not.
I'll give you something else.
fullText: You shall not have it, Diomed, faith, you shall not. I'll give you something else.

lines: It is no matter.
fullText: It is no matter.

lines: 'Twas one's that loved me better than you will.
But now you have it, take it.
fullText: 'Twas one's that loved me better than you will. But now you have it, take it.

lines: By all Diana's waiting-women yond,
And by herself, I will not tell you whose.
fullText: By all Diana's waiting-women yond, And by herself, I will not tell you whose.

lines: Well, well, 'tis done, 'tis past. And yet it is not.
I will not keep my word.
fullText: Well, well, 'tis done, 'tis past. And yet it is not. I will not keep my word.

lines: You shall not go. One cannot speak a word
But it straight starts you.
fullText: You shall not go. One cannot speak a word But it straight starts you.

lines: Ay, come.--O Jove!--Do, come.--I shall be plagued.
fullText: Ay, come.--O Jove!--Do, come.--I shall be plagued.

lines: Good night. I prithee, come.--
Troilus, farewell. One eye yet looks on thee,
But with my heart the other eye doth see.
Ah, poor our sex! This fault in us I find:
The error of our eye directs our mind.
What error leads must err. O, then conclude:
Minds swayed by eyes are full of turpitude.
fullText: Good night. I prithee, come.-- Troilus, farewell. One eye yet looks on thee, But with my heart the other eye doth see. Ah, poor our sex! This fault in us I find: The error of our eye directs our mind. What error leads must err. O, then conclude: Minds swayed by eyes are full of turpitude.
